Search results for "llvmlite function define add parameters return type example":

Title: Example—defining a simple function — llvmlite 0.49.0dev0+18.g6302ab2.dirty documentation
URL: https://llvmlite.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user-guide/ir/examples.html
Content: * Example—defining a simple function. This example defines a function that adds 2 double-precision, floating-point numbers. """ This file demonstrates a trivial function "fpadd" returning the sum oftwo floating-point numbers. """ from  llvmlite  import ir # Create some useful types double = ir. FunctionType(double,(double, double))# Create an empty module... Module(name = __file__) # and declare a function named "fpadd" inside it func = ir. Function(module, fnty, name = "fpadd") # Now implement the function block = func. append_basic_block(name = "entry") builder = ir. IRBuilder(block) a, b = func. fadd(a, b, name = "res") builder. ret(result) # Print the module IR print(module). The generated LLVM intermediate representation is printed at the end:. ; ModuleID = "examples/ir_fpadd.py" target  triple  =  "unknown-unknown-unknown" target  datalayout  =  "" define  double  @"fpadd"(double  %".1",  double  %".2"){entry:  %"res"  =  fadd  double  %".1",  %".2"  ret  double  %"res"}. To learn how to compile and execute this function, see LLVM binding layer—llvmlite.binding.

Title: COS 320 LLVMlite Documentation - cs.Princeton
URL: https://www.cs.princeton.edu/courses/archive/spring19/cos320/hw/llvmlite.shtml
Content: LLVMlite is a small subset of the LLVM IR that we will be using throughout the course as the intermediate representation in our compiler. This document explains the structure of well-formed LLVMlite programs, the semantics of LLVMlite in terms of an abstract machine, and the relevant parts of the code provided with the assignments. To give you a sense of structure of LLVMlite programs and the most basic features, the following is our running example, the simple recursive factorial function written in the conrete syntax of the LLVMlite IR. The argument is prefixed with "%" to indicate that it's an identifier local to the function, while fac is prefixed with "@" to indicate that it is in scope in the entire compilation unit. Next, at (2) we have the first instruction of the body of fac, which performs a signed comparison of the argument %n and 0 and assigns the result to the temporary %1.

"expected an integer type, got %s" %. fn = self.module.declare\_intrinsic(opname, [operand.type]). return self.call(fn, [operand], name). raise TypeError("expected a float type, got %s" % operand.type). def wrapped(self, operand, flag, name=''):. if not (isinstance(flag.type, types.IntType) and. fn = self.module.declare\_intrinsic(opname, [a.type, b.type, c.type]). opname, [operand.type, flag.type]). if a.type != b.type or b.type != c.type:. "expected types to be the same, got %s, %s, %s" % (. instr = cls(self.block, opname, val, typ, name).
